era_context: The early 18th century is shaped by the aftermath of the War of the Spanish Succession, the ongoing Great Northern War between Sweden and a coalition led by Russia, and the consolidation of Hanoverian rule in Britain. In Asia, the Qing dynasty under the aging Kangxi Emperor remains the world's most populous polity, while the Mughal Empire fragments after Aurangzeb's death in 1707. Colonial expansion in the Americas intensifies, generating violent Indigenous resistance along frontier zones. In Europe, the death of Louis XIV marks the symbolic end of an era of French hegemony.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading zones and Ethiopia
Interior Southeast Asia (Dai Viet, Lan Xang, Burmese kingdoms)
Interior South America beyond Portuguese and Spanish colonial settlements

events.title: Death of Louis XIV of France
events.description: Louis XIV died at Versailles on 1 September 1715, four days before his 77th birthday, ending the longest verified reign of any European monarch — 72 years. His death brought to a close an era that had defined European politics, warfare, and culture since 1643. His great-grandson, Louis XV, aged five, succeeded him. The immediate political consequence was the establishment of a regency under Philippe II, Duke of Orléans, who quickly moved to reverse key policies of the late king, including rapprochement with Britain and relaxation of religious persecution of Jansenists.
key_figures: Louis XIV (King of France, d. 1 September 1715)
Louis XV (successor, age 5)
Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (Regent of France)

sources.name: Saint-Simon, Louis de Rouvroy, duc de, Mémoires
Dangeau, Philippe de Courcillon, marquis de, Journal
Bluche, François, Louis XIV (1986); Pevitt, Christine, The Man Who Would Be King: The Life of Philippe d'Orléans (1997)

events.certainty_note: One of the most thoroughly documented royal deaths in European history, attested by dozens of contemporary witnesses and diarists.

events.title: Regency of Philippe d'Orléans established
events.description: On 2 September 1715, Philippe II, Duke of Orléans, secured from the Parlement of Paris full regency powers, overriding Louis XIV's will, which had attempted to limit the regent's authority through a regency council dominated by the Duke of Maine (Louis XIV's legitimised son). The Parlement annulled the restrictive provisions in exchange for the restoration of its right of remonstrance, which Louis XIV had suppressed in 1673. This bargain reshaped French governance for the next eight years, inaugurating the Régence — a period of financial experimentation, relaxed court culture, and diplomatic realignment.
key_figures: Philippe II, Duke of Orléans (Regent)
Louis Auguste de Bourbon, Duke of Maine (rival claimant to regency authority)
Parlement of Paris (collective body)

sources.name: Registers of the Parlement of Paris, September 1715
Saint-Simon, Mémoires
Shennan, J.H., Philippe, Duke of Orléans: Regent of France 1715–1723 (1979)

events.certainty_note: Documented in parliamentary registers and multiple contemporary memoirs.

events.title: Jacobite Rising of 1715 ('The Fifteen')
events.description: John Erskine, Earl of Mar, raised the Jacobite standard at Braemar on 6 September 1715, initiating the most serious attempt to restore the Stuart dynasty since the Glorious Revolution of 1688. Mar gathered a force of perhaps 10,000 Highland and northeastern Scottish supporters of the exiled James Francis Edward Stuart ('the Old Pretender'). The rising exploited widespread Scottish resentment of the 1707 Act of Union, Episcopalian hostility to Presbyterian ascendancy, and Tory disaffection with the new Hanoverian regime. However, Mar's indecisive leadership squandered his numerical advantage.
key_figures: John Erskine, 6th Earl of Mar (Jacobite commander)
James Francis Edward Stuart ('the Old Pretender')
John Campbell, 2nd Duke of Argyll (Government commander)

sources.name: Rae, Peter, The History of the Late Rebellion (1718)
Mar's correspondence and manifestos, National Archives of Scotland
Szechi, Daniel, 1715: The Great Jacobite Rebellion (2006)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both Jacobite and Hanoverian sources.

events.title: Battle of Sheriffmuir
events.description: On 13 November 1715, the Jacobite army under Mar met the smaller government force under the Duke of Argyll at Sheriffmuir. The battle was tactically inconclusive — each side's right wing routed the opposing left — but strategically it was a defeat for the Jacobites, who failed to break through to the Lowlands despite their numerical superiority. Mar retreated northward, and the rising's momentum was broken. The famous song 'There's Some Say That We Wan' captured the ambiguity of the outcome.
key_figures: John Erskine, 6th Earl of Mar (Jacobite commander)
John Campbell, 2nd Duke of Argyll (Government commander)

sources.name: Rae, Peter, The History of the Late Rebellion (1718)
Government military dispatches; Argyll's correspondence
Reid, Stuart, Sheriffmuir 1715 (2014)

events.certainty_note: Date and events well attested by both sides, though casualty figures vary between sources.

events.title: Battle of Preston and Jacobite surrender in England
events.description: On 12–14 November 1715, a combined force of English Jacobites and Scottish Highlanders under Thomas Forster and Brigadier William Mackintosh of Borlum was besieged and forced to surrender at Preston by government troops under General Charles Wills and General George Carpenter. This ended the Jacobite threat in England. The captured leaders were tried for treason; several were executed, and hundreds were transported to the American colonies. The simultaneous failure at Sheriffmuir and Preston effectively doomed the 1715 rising.
key_figures: Thomas Forster (English Jacobite commander, surrendered)
William Mackintosh of Borlum (Scottish Jacobite brigadier)
General Charles Wills (Government commander)

sources.name: Patten, Robert, The History of the Late Rebellion (1717) — written by a captured Jacobite chaplain
Government military records, National Archives (TNA)
Szechi, Daniel, 1715: The Great Jacobite Rebellion (2006)

events.certainty_note: Well documented by government records, trial proceedings, and participant accounts.

events.title: Riot Act comes into force in England
events.description: The Riot Act (1 Geo. I St. 2 c. 5), passed in 1714 in response to Jacobite-sympathising riots, came into force on 1 August 1715. It provided that any group of twelve or more persons 'unlawfully, riotously, and tumultuously assembled' must disperse within one hour of a magistrate reading the proclamation, or face felony charges. The Act became a central instrument of public order enforcement in Britain and its colonies for over two centuries, giving rise to the phrase 'reading the Riot Act.' It was not repealed until 1973.
key_figures: George I (King of Great Britain)

sources.name: 1 Geo. I St. 2 c. 5 (Riot Act, 1714), Parliamentary Archives
Hayter, Tony, The Army and the Crowd in Mid-Georgian England (1978)

events.certainty_note: Statutory text survives; enforcement records well documented.

events.title: Yamasee War begins in colonial South Carolina
events.region: British colonial South Carolina / Yamasee, Creek, and allied Indigenous peoples
events.description: On 15 April 1715 (Good Friday), Yamasee warriors attacked the English trading post at Pocotaligo, killing the South Carolina Indian agent Thomas Nairne and several other colonists. This initiated the Yamasee War, one of the most serious Indigenous-colonial conflicts in early American history. A broad coalition of Yamasee, Muscogee (Creek), Catawba, Cherokee (initially), and other nations coordinated attacks driven by grievances over trade exploitation, debt slavery, and encroachment. The war devastated the South Carolina colony, killing roughly 7% of the settler population and threatening Charleston itself before Cherokee defection from the coalition and assistance from Virginia and North Carolina turned the tide.
key_figures: Thomas Nairne (South Carolina Indian agent, killed)
Charles Craven (Governor of South Carolina)
Yamasee, Creek, and Catawba war leaders (names largely unrecorded in English sources)

sources.name: South Carolina Commons House of Assembly journals, 1715
Letters of Governor Craven and South Carolina officials to the Board of Trade
Ramsey, William, The Yamasee War: A Study of Culture, Economy, and Conflict in the Colonial South (2008)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in colonial administrative records, though Indigenous perspectives are filtered through English sources.

events.title: Spanish Treasure Fleet wrecked off Florida coast
events.region: Spanish Empire / Caribbean (east coast of Florida)
events.description: On 31 July 1715, a combined fleet of eleven Spanish ships (the Tierra Firme and New Spain fleets) carrying silver, gold, and trade goods estimated at 14 million pesos was struck by a hurricane off the east coast of Florida near present-day Vero Beach. All but one ship sank, and approximately 1,000 sailors drowned. The disaster dealt a severe blow to the Spanish treasury, already weakened by the War of the Spanish Succession. Salvage efforts began immediately and continued for years; the wrecks have yielded significant archaeological finds into the 21st century and remain among the most famous shipwreck sites in the Americas.
key_figures: Juan Esteban de Ubilla (commander of the New Spain fleet, killed)
Antonio de Echeverz y Zubiza (commander of the Tierra Firme fleet, survived)

sources.name: Spanish colonial correspondence, Archivo General de Indias (Seville)
Burgess, Robert F. and Clausen, Carl J., Florida's Golden Galleons: The Search for the 1715 Spanish Treasure Fleet (1982)
Archaeological evidence from ongoing wreck site excavations

events.certainty_note: Documented in Spanish colonial records and confirmed by extensive underwater archaeology.

events.title: Halley's solar eclipse map — first scientific prediction of a total eclipse path across England
events.description: On 3 May 1715 (OS 22 April), a total solar eclipse crossed southern England and northern Europe. Edmond Halley predicted the path of totality in advance and published a map in advance of the event, soliciting public observations to refine his calculations. This was the first time a total solar eclipse path had been accurately predicted and mapped for public use. Halley subsequently published a corrected map comparing his prediction with the observed path, demonstrating the power of Newtonian gravitational theory for astronomical prediction. The eclipse was widely observed and recorded across England.
key_figures: Edmond Halley (Savilian Professor of Geometry, Oxford)

sources.name: Halley, Edmond, 'Observations of the Late Total Eclipse of the Sun,' Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, Vol. 29 (1715)
Halley's pre-eclipse broadside map (1715), Royal Society archives
Pasachoff, Jay M. and Olson, Roberta J.M., studies on the 1715 eclipse observation campaign

events.certainty_note: Halley's published map and subsequent correction are preserved in the Royal Society's archives.

events.title: Papal bull Ex illa die against Chinese Rites
events.description: On 19 March 1715, Pope Clement XI issued the apostolic constitution Ex illa die, definitively condemning the practice of accommodating Chinese ancestral rites and Confucian ceremonies within Catholic worship — the so-called 'Chinese Rites Controversy.' The bull required all missionaries in China to swear an oath rejecting accommodation. This directly contradicted the tolerant approach of most Jesuit missionaries and enraged the Kangxi Emperor, who had issued an edict of toleration for Christianity in 1692 on the understanding that Catholic practice would not forbid ancestor veneration. The bull severely damaged the Catholic mission in China and contributed to the Kangxi Emperor's increasing restriction of missionary activity.
key_figures: Pope Clement XI (issuer of the bull)
Kangxi Emperor (Qing ruler who opposed the decision)
Charles-Thomas Maillard de Tournon (papal legate who had enforced the ban, d. 1710)

sources.name: Papal constitution Ex illa die (19 March 1715), Vatican Archives
Mungello, David E., The Chinese Rites Controversy: Its History and Meaning (1994)
Standaert, Nicolas (ed.), Handbook of Christianity in China, Vol. 1 (2001)

events.certainty_note: The papal text is preserved; its reception in China documented in both Jesuit and Qing sources.

events.title: Treaty of Greifswald — Prussia and Hanover enter the Great Northern War against Sweden
events.description: In April 1715, Prussia and Hanover formally joined the anti-Swedish coalition in the Great Northern War through the Treaty of Greifswald with Denmark. In November, Prussian and Danish forces landed on the island of Rügen at the Battle of Stresow (15 November), defeating the Swedish garrison and forcing Charles XII's deputy to withdraw. This marked the effective loss of Sweden's last major foothold in German Pomerania and further isolated Sweden in the war. Hanover's entry was partly motivated by George I's desire to acquire the former Swedish territories of Bremen and Verden for his electorate.
key_figures: Frederick William I (King of Prussia)
George I (King of Great Britain and Elector of Hanover)
Frederick IV (King of Denmark)
Charles XII (King of Sweden, absent in Ottoman exile until 1714, now returned)

sources.name: Treaty text; Prussian and Danish diplomatic correspondence
Frost, Robert I., The Northern Wars: War, State and Society in Northeastern Europe, 1558–1721 (2000)

events.certainty_note: Treaty and military operations well documented in Prussian, Danish, and Swedish archives.

events.title: Siege and fall of Stralsund to anti-Swedish coalition
events.description: Beginning in mid-1715, Prussian, Danish, and Saxon forces besieged the Swedish fortress city of Stralsund, the principal Swedish stronghold in Pomerania. Charles XII himself was present in the city, having returned from his Ottoman exile. After the loss of Rügen in November, the position became untenable. Charles XII escaped by ship on 22 December 1715, leaving the garrison to surrender. The fall of Stralsund effectively ended Swedish control of its German territories and marked the twilight of Sweden's Baltic empire.
key_figures: Charles XII of Sweden (escaped the siege by boat)
Frederick William I of Prussia (besieging power)
Frederick IV of Denmark (besieging power)

sources.name: Swedish and Prussian military correspondence and siege records
Hatton, Ragnhild, Charles XII of Sweden (1968)

events.certainty_note: Charles XII's escape and the subsequent surrender are precisely dated in multiple independent sources.

events.title: Sayyid Brothers consolidate power in the Mughal Empire
events.description: In 1715, the Sayyid Brothers — Husain Ali Khan (governor of the Deccan and later Mir Bakshi) and Abdullah Khan (Wazir) — were emerging as the dominant political force in the Mughal Empire, effectively becoming kingmakers. Emperor Farrukhsiyar, enthroned in 1713 with their support, was increasingly at odds with them. The Sayyids' power reflected the broader fragmentation of Mughal central authority after Aurangzeb's death in 1707, as regional governors, Maratha chiefs, and Rajput rulers asserted greater autonomy. This period is often characterised as the beginning of the Mughal Empire's transformation into a nominal sovereignty.
key_figures: Husain Ali Khan (Sayyid Brother, military commander)
Abdullah Khan (Sayyid Brother, Wazir)
Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor, r. 1713–1719)

sources.name: Khafi Khan, Muntakhab al-Lubab (court chronicle)
Richards, John F., The Mughal Empire (New Cambridge History of India, 1993)

events.certainty_note: Mughal court chronicles and European trading company records document the Sayyid Brothers' ascendancy in detail.

events.title: Farrukhsiyar's farman grants trading privileges to the British East India Company
events.description: In 1715, Mughal Emperor Farrukhsiyar issued a farman (imperial decree) granting the British East India Company significant trading concessions, including duty-free trade in Bengal, the right to mint coins in Bombay, and additional territory around Madras. The Company's embassy, led by John Surman, had arrived at the Mughal court in 1714. The farman was sometimes called the 'Magna Carta of the Company' by later British writers, though its immediate impact was contested — Mughal provincial officials often obstructed its implementation. Nevertheless, it provided the legal basis for the Company's expanding commercial and eventually territorial claims in India.
key_figures: Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor)
John Surman (East India Company ambassador)
William Hamilton (Company surgeon who treated the Emperor)

sources.name: East India Company factory records and correspondence, British Library (India Office Records)
Chaudhuri, K.N., The Trading World of Asia and the English East India Company 1660–1760 (1978)

events.certainty_note: The farman's text survives; its date is sometimes given as 1716 in some sources depending on when the final version was confirmed, but the embassy and initial grant are firmly in 1715.

events.title: First Maratha raids into the Mughal Deccan provinces
events.description: By 1715, Maratha chiefs under the Peshwa and various sardars were conducting increasingly aggressive raids (chauth and sardeshmukhi collection) across the Deccan, exploiting the power vacuum left by the weakening of Mughal provincial authority. Balaji Vishwanath, appointed Peshwa in 1713, was consolidating Maratha political organisation and would soon negotiate directly with the Sayyid Brothers. These raids marked the transition of the Maratha Confederacy from a regional power to a subcontinent-spanning force that would dominate 18th-century Indian politics.
key_figures: Balaji Vishwanath (Peshwa of the Maratha Confederacy)
Shahu I (Chhatrapati of the Maratha Empire)

sources.name: Maratha administrative documents (Modi script records); Mughal provincial correspondence
Gordon, Stewart, The Marathas 1600–1818 (New Cambridge History of India, 1993)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern is well attested; specific raid dates and locations in 1715 are approximate, drawn from cumulative Maratha and Mughal records.

events.title: Philip V of Spain reconquers Mallorca — end of the War of the Spanish Succession in Iberia
events.description: On 11 July 1715, Bourbon forces under the Chevalier d'Asfeld completed the conquest of Mallorca, the last holdout of Archduke Charles's (now Emperor Charles VI) supporters in Spain. Mallorca had maintained allegiance to the Habsburg cause even after the fall of Barcelona in September 1714. The conquest ended all armed resistance to Philip V's rule in the Spanish kingdoms and completed the Bourbon consolidation of Spain. The Nueva Planta decrees were subsequently extended to the Balearic Islands, abolishing their traditional fueros and institutions.
key_figures: Philip V (King of Spain)
Chevalier d'Asfeld (French-born Bourbon commander)
Juan Sureda i de Villalonga (Mallorcan resistance leader)

sources.name: Spanish royal correspondence and military records, Archivo General de Simancas
Kamen, Henry, The War of Succession in Spain, 1700–15 (1969)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in Spanish and French military records.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor confronts the papal ban on Chinese Rites
events.description: Following the promulgation of Ex illa die, the Kangxi Emperor responded with increasing hostility toward missionaries who refused to follow the earlier Jesuit accommodation of Chinese rites. The Emperor had previously shown favour to Jesuits who accepted ancestor veneration as a civil rather than religious practice. The papal condemnation was seen at the Qing court as an intolerable interference in Chinese cultural sovereignty. Kangxi ordered that only missionaries holding the earlier tolerance permit (the piao) could remain in China, effectively expelling non-accommodationist missionaries. This marked a decisive turn toward restriction of Christian activity in China.
key_figures: Kangxi Emperor (Qing ruler, r. 1661–1722)

sources.name: Kangxi Emperor's edicts on the missionary question; Jesuit correspondence from Beijing
Mungello, David E., The Chinese Rites Controversy (1994)

events.certainty_note: Documented in both Chinese imperial records and Jesuit missionary correspondence.

events.title: Brook Taylor publishes Methodus Incrementorum Directa et Inversa
events.description: In 1715, English mathematician Brook Taylor published Methodus Incrementorum Directa et Inversa, which contained the first published statement of what became known as Taylor's theorem — the expansion of a function as an infinite series around a point. Although the result had been partially anticipated by James Gregory, Johann Bernoulli, and others, Taylor's formulation became the standard reference. The work also contained contributions to the calculus of finite differences and the theory of vibrating strings. Taylor's theorem became a foundational tool of mathematical analysis.
key_figures: Brook Taylor (mathematician, FRS)

sources.name: Taylor, Brook, Methodus Incrementorum Directa et Inversa (London, 1715)
Feigenbaum, Lenore, 'Brook Taylor and the Method of Increments,' Archive for History of Exact Sciences, Vol. 34 (1985)

events.certainty_note: The publication date is firm; the book survives in multiple copies.

events.title: Japanese shogunal succession crisis — death of Tokugawa Ietsugu
events.description: The child shogun Tokugawa Ietsugu, who had succeeded at age three in 1713, remained a figurehead under the regency of senior councillors. The real power behind the shogunate was the controversial chamberlain Manabe Akifusa and the former advisor Arai Hakuseki, who had driven the Shotoku reforms. Ietsugu's fragile health was a matter of concern throughout 1715 — he would die in 1716 at age seven, ending the main Tokugawa bloodline and triggering the elevation of Tokugawa Yoshimune from the Kii branch. The uncertainty over succession shaped factional politics at Edo throughout the year.
key_figures: Tokugawa Ietsugu (Shogun, child)
Manabe Akifusa (senior chamberlain)
Arai Hakuseki (Confucian scholar-advisor)

sources.name: Tokugawa jikki (official Tokugawa chronicle, compiled later from contemporary records)
Totman, Conrad, Politics in the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1600–1843 (1967)

events.certainty_note: Bakufu administrative records survive, though filtered through later compilation.

events.title: Ethiopian Emperor Dawit III consolidates rule after succession struggle
events.description: In the Ethiopian Empire, the early 18th century was marked by frequent succession crises in the Solomonic dynasty, with powerful ras (regional lords) and the oromo military aristocracy increasingly dictating the choice of emperor. Emperor Dawit III (r. 1716–1721) was approaching power in this period, while the preceding emperor Yostos (r. 1711–1716) struggled to maintain authority against factional opposition in Gondar. Ethiopian court chronicles record intense political competition among the great nobles, foreshadowing the Zemene Mesafint ('Era of the Princes') that would soon paralyse central authority.
key_figures: Yostos (Emperor of Ethiopia, r. 1711–1716)
Various ras and regional lords

sources.name: Royal chronicles of Gondar (Ge'ez manuscripts)
Abir, Mordechai, Ethiopia: The Era of the Princes (1968)

events.certainty_note: Ethiopian royal chronicles provide the main narrative, but precise dating of political events in this period can be uncertain by a year or two.

events.title: First Barrier Treaty between the Dutch Republic and Austria
events.description: On 15 November 1715, the Third Barrier Treaty was signed between the Dutch Republic and the Habsburg Emperor Charles VI, establishing the terms under which the Dutch would garrison a chain of fortresses in the Austrian (formerly Spanish) Netherlands as a defensive barrier against France. The treaty was a key consequence of the War of the Spanish Succession and the Treaties of Utrecht and Rastatt. The Austrian side resented the financial terms, which required them to subsidise Dutch garrisons on their own territory, while the Dutch saw the barrier as essential to their security. The arrangement shaped the geopolitics of the Low Countries until the French Revolutionary Wars.
key_figures: Charles VI (Holy Roman Emperor)
Anthonie Heinsius (Grand Pensionary of Holland)
George I (King of Great Britain, guarantor)

sources.name: Treaty text, published in Dumont, Corps universel diplomatique
Geyl, Pieter, The Netherlands in the Seventeenth Century (1961–1964); Dhondt, F., studies on the Barrier Treaties

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives; date and terms well documented in diplomatic archives.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of scholarly dispute affect events listed above. (1) The Farrukhsiyar farman (1715-014): The traditional British characterisation of this decree as the 'Magna Carta of the East India Company' is rejected by modern Indian historiography as anachronistic and teleological. Scholars such as Sanjay Subrahmanyam argue it was one of many such farmans granted to European trading companies, was frequently ignored by Mughal provincial officials, and did not confer meaningful sovereignty. Its later significance was a product of British imperial expansion, not inherent in the document itself. (2) Halley's eclipse map (1715-009): While rightly celebrated, some historians of science note that the prediction's accuracy has been somewhat mythologised — Halley's predicted path of totality was roughly 20 miles off in several places, and he acknowledged this himself in his corrected post-eclipse map. (3) The Jacobite Rising (1715-003 through 1715-005): Recent scholarship (Szechi, 2006; Pittock, 2009) has challenged the older Whig historiographic view that the Fifteen was a quixotic, doomed enterprise. These historians argue the rising had a realistic chance of success given the unpopularity of the Hanoverian succession, and that its failure was primarily due to Mar's incompetent leadership rather than structural inevitability. (4) Taylor's theorem (1715-018): Priority disputes exist — Johann Bernoulli had communicated similar results earlier, and James Gregory had versions of the expansion in the 1670s. Taylor's originality lies more in systematic presentation than in the core mathematical insight.
historiographic_note: 1715 is an exceptionally well-documented year in European history, with extensive state archives, diplomatic correspondence, personal memoirs, and printed literature surviving from France, Britain, the German states, Spain, and the Dutch Republic. For the Mughal Empire, court chronicles (such as Khafi Khan) and European trading company records provide parallel but often contradictory narratives. For the Qing Empire, imperial edicts, Jesuit correspondence, and provincial records offer rich documentation, though the Chinese Rites controversy is disproportionately represented through the European missionary lens. The Yamasee War is documented primarily through colonial administrative records; Indigenous perspectives are almost entirely mediated through English sources. Ethiopian events rely heavily on royal chronicles composed in Ge'ez at Gondar, with limited external corroboration. Sub-Saharan Africa beyond Ethiopia, Oceania, and much of Central and Southeast Asia remain poorly documented in readily accessible sources for this year, reflecting both genuine gaps in the surviving record and biases in modern historiographic training data.

graph_edges.note: Louis XIV's death immediately triggered the regency crisis and Orléans' seizure of full powers
Sheriffmuir was the decisive battle of the Jacobite Rising of 1715
Preston was the English theatre of the same Jacobite Rising
The Riot Act was passed partly in response to Jacobite-sympathising riots preceding and during the rising
The papal bull Ex illa die provoked the Kangxi Emperor's restrictive response
The Treaty of Greifswald and the loss of Rügen made the siege of Stralsund untenable for Sweden
The Sayyid Brothers' dominance and Mughal fragmentation formed the context in which Farrukhsiyar granted EIC concessions
Mughal weakness under the Sayyid Brothers enabled Maratha expansion in the Deccan
The conquest of Mallorca completed the military resolution of the War of the Spanish Succession
Louis XIV's death came two years after the Treaty of Utrecht ended the war that defined his later reign
The Yamasee War continued through 1717, reshaping colonial-Indigenous relations across the Southeast
The farman's trading privileges were later invoked as legal basis for EIC expansion leading to Plassey
Loss of Stralsund forced Charles XII back to Sweden, where he launched the Norway campaign ending in his death
